rationale: This is a classic textbook example of the Houben-Hoesch reaction, demonstrating the acylation of a highly activated, electron-rich aromatic ring (1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene) with a simple nitrile (acetonitrile). The reaction is catalyzed by a Lewis acid (ZnCl2) and a Brønsted acid (HCl), leading to the formation of an aryl ketone after hydrolysis of the intermediate ketimine. The reactants and product are simple, acyclic where possible, and clearly illustrate the core transformation of this named reaction.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses a highly activated polyalkoxybenzene, which is characteristic of the Houben-Hoesch reaction's substrate scope. It avoids simple phenols (which could collide with Fries or Kolbe-Schmitt) and generic arenes (which could collide with Friedel-Crafts acylation). The use of a nitrile as the acylating agent is the defining feature, distinguishing it from reactions using acyl halides or anhydrides.
